coredata.dc:title: Surface-controlled carbon dots with high brightness and tunable emission towards fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and pH sensors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yi
Wang, Shuangyu
Zhang, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Compared with luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals, nontoxic and metal-free carbon dots (CDs) are advantages for the significant applications in displaying and various sensors. In this paper, blue- and green-emitting CDs with high brightness and stability were created via solvothermal synthesis using m-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid. Solvents were adjusted to control the surface of CDs attaining high brightness. The blue-emitting CDs with a photoluminescence (PL) peak wavelength of 480 nm revealed PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of 30.4% while the green-emitting one revealed a PL peak wavelength of 500 nm and a PLQY of 55.1%. The PL properties of these CDs exhibited excellent pH dependence. The effects of solvents, synthetic parameters, and pH values on the structure and PL properties of CDs were tested. These CDs were used for pH sensors and multi-stage anti-counterfeiting ink. The blue-emitting CDs revealed high stability in a broad pH range while green-emitting CDs were sensitive to aqueous solution in the pH region of 10.00–10.78 with a sensitivity of 0.99/pH and pKa value of 10.34 ± 0.51. Because of the high stability, fluorescent inks were prepared using these multi-color CDs via direct dispersion in water, in which the inks showed high stability after 6 months of storage time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
pH sensors
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of samples. (a, b) Sample b-CDs. (c, d) Sample g-CDs. The insets in (b) and (d) show the size distribution of b-CDs and g-CDs.
(a-d) XPS spectra of b-CDs and g-CDs: (a) Survey spectra, (b) C1s spectra, (c) O1s spectra, (d) N1s spectra. (e) FT-IR spectra of b-CDs and g-CDs. (f) XRD patterns of b-CDs and g-CDs.

(a) PL spectra of g-CDs dispersed in water of different pH value. (b) Integral area and FWHM of PL spectra for g-CDs in different pH solution. (c) Evolution of PL spectra upon pH change for g-CDs in alkaline media. (d) Fluorescence response cyclic ability of g-CDs in extreme acid-base media.

(a-c) Picture of multicolor CDs inks under (a) room light, (b) UV light, (c) after storing for 6 months. (d-e) picture of coded number with b-CDs and g-CDs under (d) UV light, (e)after spraying using HCl solution, (f) after spraying using NaOH solution.
Synthesis process of samples b-CDs and g-CDs.
Preparation conditions and PL properties of typical bright CDs samples.
Synthesis conditions and properties of CDs using different reaction media. 1

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction condition: 0.025 g of m-phenylenediamine, 0.100 g of terephthalic acid, 10 mL of reaction medium at 180℃ for 12 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Time constants τ1, τ2, τ3, and components B1, B2, B3 and average lifetime (τavg) of the CDs samples.

head.ce:title: Surface-controlled carbon dots with high brightness and tunable emission towards fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and pH sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared with luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals, nontoxic and metal-free carbon dots (CDs) are advantages for the significant applications in displaying and various sensors. In this paper, blue- and green-emitting CDs with high brightness and stability were created via solvothermal synthesis using m-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid. Solvents were adjusted to control the surface of CDs attaining high brightness. The blue-emitting CDs with a photoluminescence (PL) peak wavelength of 480 nm revealed PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of 30.4% while the green-emitting one revealed a PL peak wavelength of 500 nm and a PLQY of 55.1%. The PL properties of these CDs exhibited excellent pH dependence. The effects of solvents, synthetic parameters, and pH values on the structure and PL properties of CDs were tested. These CDs were used for pH sensors and multi-stage anti-counterfeiting ink. The blue-emitting CDs revealed high stability in a broad pH range while green-emitting CDs were sensitive to aqueous solution in the pH region of 10.00–10.78 with a sensitivity of 0.99/pH and pKa value of 10.34 ± 0.51. Because of the high stability, fluorescent inks were prepared using these multi-color CDs via direct dispersion in water, in which the inks showed high stability after 6 months of storage time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with traditional analytical methods such as electrochemical sensors, fluorescence sensors for pH measurement have attracted much attention due to the advantages such as high sensitivity, reversible, rapid, and continuous measurement [1]. Especially the detection of alkaline pH is particularly important in many fields such as bio acid-base imbalance [2], concrete quality monitoring, metal mining and other fields [3]. However, they are still disadvantaged by their high toxicity, poor stability, complex synthetic processes, and high starting material cost. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, low preparation cost, good biocompatibility, stability, and have broad application prospects [1,4–6]. At present, the research of CDs as pH indicators is also booming [7]. However, most CDs or fluorescent probes are pH sensitive in the acidic or neutral range, and few CDs can be applied to alkaline pH detection without the aid of alkali-sensitive fluorescein [8–11]. This is because alkaline environments often cause changes in properties such as functional groups and aggregation states on the surface of CDs [8,12]. Moreover, based on literature reports, there are still issues encountered in this topic, such as small detection range, poor repeatability, and unknown quenching mechanism.

The utilization of photoluminescent inks has emerged as one of the most captivating techniques within the realm of anti-counterfeiting [13,14]. Traditional anti-counterfeiting materials often rely on complex graded coding, substrate assistance, and toxic oil-based dyes, which often limits their applications [15–20]. Wang et al. suppressed the fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation by placing alky chain grafting on the surface of CDs and developed a solve-free direct writing fluorescent ink; however, this method required additional post-modification and purification steps [20]. Thus, it is important to develop easy-to-operate, safe and stable water-based inks that can realize hierarchical encoding by pH control.

The solvothermal method has become one of the most significant methods for the preparation of CDs. In addition to the preparation of a small amounts of solvent-free CDs, most of the preparation methodologies require the use of solvent, which has been considered one of the most important factors that affect the structure and optical properties of the CDs. Currently, research on solvent engineering in the field of CDs are often divided into two major aspects: solvent control that examines the effects of reaction medium on CDs during the reaction processes; and solvent response that investigates the impacts of dispersed solvents on CDs in various application [21]. There are numerous articles reporting on solvent-dependence of the material, yet the importance of solvent control has been overlooked. Reaction media often can affect the CDs by altering elemental composition, functional groups, surface states, and sp2 conjugate domain size [22,23]. However, studies on the effects of these on the morphology, structure, and aggregation state of the CDs, as well as the effects of solvents on CDs properties are still urgently needed [21].

In this work, blue- and green-emitting CDs with photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of 30.4 and 55.1% were prepared using terephthalic acid (TA) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) as precursors and formamide and ethanol as solvents. It was found that the CDs sp2 domain was clearly observed when formamide was used as the solvent; nitrogen existed in the form of pyridinic nitrogen; and the CDs were closely contacted. When ethanol was used as the solvent, the CDs’ size was smaller with no clear lattice fringes, the CDs were wrapped in polymer chains, and the nitrogen elements existed in different forms. The CDs prepared using ethanol as solvent were sensitive to pH response in alkaline media and could accurately detect in pH = 10–11 region. By replacing mPD in precursors with oPD, yellow and red CDs were prepared. Multicolor-emitting inks were successfully prepared by mixing CDs with deionized water. The sensitivity of green CDs in alkaline pH was able to attain graded anti-counterfeiting. The fluorescent writing was preserved even after the paper been exposed to air at room temperature for two months.

The m-phenylenediamine (mPD, 99.5%), o-phenylenediamine (oPD, 98.5%), terephthalic acid (TA, 99%), and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., LTD. Other organic solvents were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and were used directly. The deionized water used in experimental was obtained by a MERCK MILLIPORE system (18.2 MΩ cm).

In a typical g-CDs preparation process, 0.083 g of mPD and 0.042 g of TA were added to 10 mL of ethanol with ultrasound for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. The suspension was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined high-pressure reactor and heated at 180°C for 12 h. After the reaction, the sample was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged to obtain the precipitation (10000 rpm, 5 min). The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filtration membrane to remove large particles. To test the fluorescence properties of CDs, 10 μL solution was diluted to 2 mL (and the diluted solvent used was the same as the reaction medium, with a CDs concentration of 62.5 mg/L). For the synthesis of b-CDs, ethanol was replaced with formamide, but similar synthesis procedure was used. To study the effects of solvents on surface of CDs, various solvents were used including ethyl acetate (EA), methylene chloride (DCM), N, N dimethylamide (DMF), or water, and the prepared CDs were named EA-CDs, DCM-CDs, DMF-CDs, H2O-CDs respectively. In case of absorption spectra comparison, the CDs prepared using formamide and ethanol as the solvents were named as b-CDs and g-CDs, respectively. Detailed preparation conditions of the samples are illustrated in Table 1 .

The morphologies of samples were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL 2100, Japan). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using D8-ADVANCE (Bruker Corporation, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA) with the samples being irradiated by monochromatic Al K at 1486.6 eV energy level (12 kV; 6 mA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using VG Micro Tech ESCA 300 (Thermo Electron, the USA) infrared spectrophotometer using the KBr tablet technique. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-4100, Japan). PL spectra were recorded from a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan) while fluorescence decay plots were obtained from Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan). The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was determined using Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ x
= Φ st
Grad x
/ Gradst
∗
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
where, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield; Grad denotes the slope of the integrated area of the emission peak for the absorbance. The subscripts X and ST denote the cCDs and reference sample rhodamine 6G (QY = 98% in ethanol), respectively. η denotes the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs solutions with different pH values and the calculation of sensitivity and pKa
ce:section.ce:para:
A certain amount of 0.1 M NaOH solution and 0.1 M HCl solution was added to deionized water to adjust the pH of the solution to the required value. The pH values were tested by a calibrated acidometer (Hangzhou Qiwei Instrument Co., LTD. PHB-1). 10 μL of CDs solution (with a concentration of 62.5 mg/L) was added to 2 mL of deionized water with desired pH value for the test (3.125 × 10−4 mg/mL). The sensitivity was calculated using Eq. (2).
(2)
Sensitivity = ( Δ I /
I 0
) / Δ p H

I and I0 represent the integral areas of the PL spectra of CDs at different pH and neutral pH value, respectively. ΔpH and ΔI represent the amount of change in pH value and the change in the integral area of the corresponding CDs, respectively. The equilibrium between the protonated (HA) and deprotonated (A−) can be expressed by Eq. (3)
(3)
HA ↔
H
+
+
A
-

According to the Henderson-Hasselbalch eq., the pH value is equal to the logarithm of the acid-base equilibrium constant pKa plus the ratio of the concentrations of the protonated and deprotonated forms of the substance. The concentrations of A− and HA can be expressed by “
F -
F
max
” and “
F
min
- F
” respectively. F represents the integral area of the tested fluorescence spectrum,
F
max
and
F
min
represent the fluorescence area of all protonated systems and all deprotonated systems, respectively. Therefore, the relationship between pH and fluorescence intensity can be expressed by Eqs. (4) and (5).
(4)
pH = p K a + l o g
F -
F
max
F
min
- F
(5)
F =
F
max
+
F
min
×
10
( p H - p K a )
10
( p H - p K a )
+ 1

By measuring the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum of CDs under different pH conditions and nonlinear fitting according to Eq. (5), an “S” shaped curve can be obtained. The curve is centered on the pKa value, which is the pH value when the degree of protonation of the CDs is 50%.

Blue- and green-fluorescent inks were prepared by diluting 0.5 mL of the CDs solution in 3 mL of deionized water with a CDs concentration of 1.786 mg/mL. A slide glass was used to dip the fluorescent ink and write on the weighing paper, and after the ink dried, photographs of the weighing paper were taken under room light and UV light. The preparation process of yellow-emitting ink and red-emitting ink was very similar to that of green-emitting ink, except that mPD was replaced by oPD in the raw material for CDs preparation.

Scheme 1 illustrates the typical preparation processes of bright blue- and green-emitting CDs. Because of different precursors and solvents, the surface situation of the CDs can be adjusted. The TEM images of CDs prepared with different precursors and solvents are presented in Fig. 1 . Both sample b-CDs and g-CDs revealed poor crystallinity. Because the carbon cores of CDs were wrapped in polymer chains and had aggregation behavior, there were no distinct lattice fringes and single particle dispersion structures [24,25]. The distribution patterns of the b-CDs in Fig. 1a and 1b differed from those of the g-CDs in Fig. 1c and 1d. Fig. 1b shows linear arrangements of b-CDs, corresponding to the head-to-head J-aggregate structures. The average size of b-CDs was 10.4 ± 0.13 nm. The g-CDs in Fig. 1c and 1d displays a loose and amorphous aggregation structures, suggesting various aggregation behaviors. The average size of g-CDs was 13.3 ± 0.08 nm, slightly larger than that of b-CDs. Furthermore, the b-CDs exhibited darker aggregation structures, indicating that their aggregation structures were more compact. Considering that the polymer chain length affects the kinetic radius of the CDs in solution, this phenomenon may indicate that b-CDs process shorter polymer chains, while g-CDs revealed longer polymer chains. This was related to the viscosity and polarity of reaction media. The high viscosity formamide had a domain- limiting effect on the growth of b-CDs, and limited their aggregation space. Formamide possesses large dipole moment and can form hydrogen bonds with surface functional groups of b-CDs, resulting in strong interaction between the components, preventing the free diffusion to form large aggregates. In contrast, since ethanol has low boiling point and low viscosity, it is spatially favorable for the full growth and free polymerization of the g-CDs polymer chain. Moreover, formamide was more involved in the formation of the core structure in the CDs in this case, while ethanol facilitated the formation of the polymer chain shell of the CDs [26].

Fig. 2 a shows the XPS survey spectra of samples, in which both b-CDs and g-CDs revealed elements C, N, and O. The oxygen component of g-CDs was significantly higher than that of b-CDs, and the oxygen content of g-CDs is twice that of b-CDs, while the nitrogen content remains basically unchanged. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 2b confirms the existence of multiple chemical states of element C, which are C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.2 eV) and CO (288.1 eV) [27]. Sample g-CDs have higher C-O/C-N peaks, corresponding to more C-O structure in the CDs. In addition, the CO of the green CDs moves about 1 eV in the direction of high binding energy, indicating that the oxidation degree of the g-CDs CO is higher, corresponding to the binding energy of O-CO [28]. In the O1s spectrum in Fig. 2c, the peaks of b-CDs at 531.5 and 533.5 eV correspond to CO and C-O, respectively [27]. The chemical environment of oxygen in g-CDs is richer, including O-H (531.0 eV), CO (532.1 eV), and C-O (533.4 eV) [29]. This means that the surface of the g-CDs is hydroxylated or has more defective oxygen. The movement of CO towards higher binding energy also further proves that g-CDs have a higher oxidation degree, and it is inferred that g-CDs form more carboxyl functional groups. In Fig. 2d, the b-CDs only have the chemical state of pyridinc nitrogen at 399.5 eV, while the g-CDs have pyridinium nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen at 399.2, 399.8 and 400.8 eV, respectively. This shows that the type of solvent has a great influence on the chemical environment of nitrogen in the synthesized CDs during the solvothermal process. The abundant carbonyl group in formamide can bind with the carbon adjacent to graphitic nitrogen to trigger the ring-opening of the C-N bond so that the graphitic nitrogen can be converted to the highly active pyridinic nitrogen.

In Fig. 2e, the peaks at 3550, 341 and 3000 cm−1 correspond to N-H, O-H, and C-H, respectively. The more prominent O-H peak of the g-CDs confirmed the judgment that the surface of the g-CDs was hydroxylated in the XPS analysis. The oxidation process converts the CO group into COOH, indicating that the surface of g-CDs contains a large number of carboxylic acids at terminal groups. The peak of the b-CDs at 1683 cm−1 and the peak of the g-CDs at 1635 cm−1 are both attributed to CO. The CO and the C-N bond at 1398 cm−1 form the amide bond [30]. At 1616, 1313 and 951 cm−1, the b-CDs and the g-CDs have strong CC, C-O stretching vibration and C-O-H out-of-plane bending vibration of carboxylic acid. The b-CDs has an additional C-O-C stretching peak at 1051 cm−1 [31].

The XRD patterns of CDs in Fig. 2f show a peak that the CDs consist of a graphitized core and a non-graphitized surface polymer chain. The CDs have a wide peak at 2θ = 15-30°, corresponding to amorphous carbon. The peak of 2θ = 24.5° comes from the (002) crystal face of graphitic carbon [32]. Peaks centered on 2θ = 16° are derived from highly disordered carbon and polymer chains [33]. The peak of g-CDs at 2θ = 16° is significantly higher than that of b-CDs, which proves that g-CDs contain a more disordered chain structure. Small peaks of g-CDs at 13.1, 17.5, and 27.3° are also associated with pegylated polymer chains [26].

Furthermore, the Raman spectra in Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information) can also confirm the structural differences between b-CDs and g-CDs. Band G at 1583 cm−1 and band D at 1357 cm−1 correspond to the sp2 graphitization structure and lattice defects of CDs respectively. The larger D peak of g-CDs indicates that the g-CDs have more lattice defects, while the b-CDs have a higher graphitization degree. However, the peak of the b-CDs at 1583 cm−1 is not obvious, which is influenced by the C = O of p-benzoquinone or the N-H vibration peak of the aromatic amine at 1626 cm−1 [34]. Moreover, the abundant Raman peaks revealed abundant C = C bonds and C-N bonds in CDs, indicating that para-benzoquinone and phenazine-like structures may be formed in CDs. For detailed analysis, see Table S1 in Supporting Information.

To systematically study the effect of reaction medium on the fluorescence properties of CDs, a series of CDs were prepared using six reaction media as indicated in Table 2 . Fig. S2a and S2b show that the fluorescence spectra of CDs are significantly dependent on the reaction media chosen, and the emission wavelength ranges from violet to green (432–514 nm). When ethyl acetate and dichloromethane are used as reaction media, the PLQY is very low, especially the PLQY of the DCM-CDs is only 0.6%. The PLQY increases with the increase of media polarity. The polymer layer on the surface of the CDs can block the entry of highly polar medium molecules, thereby maintaining its luminescence center, while low-polarity medium molecules, especially chlorine-containing solvents, have higher diffusion coefficients and solubility, and can penetrate the polymer interior, resulting in the separation of the luminescence center, thereby changing its fluorescence spectrum [35].

In general, DMF can be involved in the formation of sp2 hybrid structures of carbon core, which often increased the graphitic nitrogen content for redshifted emission. Compared with DMF, formamide has a higher N-reactivity, which is more conducive to the production of graphitic nitrogen and redshift of emission [36]. However, DMF-CDs and Formamide-CDs did not show significant redshift. It was clear that the conventional solvent control theory could not explain the phenomenon observed in this case. Factors such as solvent-induced aggregation were required to be considered. The narrower full width at half maximum (FWHM) and larger Stokes shift also confirmed that Formamide-CDs contained more graphitic nitrogen, which in turn formed CDs with higher carbonization and emission redshift. The FWHM of EtOH-CDs and H2O-CDs prepared by highly polar media is the narrowest, about 70 nm, indicating that protonated media are beneficial to the monochromaticity of CDs. The utilization of ethanol or water media also showed similar excitation and absorption spectra in CDs, indicating the formation of analogous structure. However, the PLQY of EtOH-CDs was higher than H2O-CDs, most likely because EtOH-CDs were more dehydrated and formed polyethylene glycol-like polymer chains. The smaller Stokes shift of EtOH-CDs also demonstrated the material to have less electron transfer energy loss and fewer defect states.

To further explore the influence of solvent control on CDs, Fig. S2c and Fig. S3 (see Supporting Information) show the absorption spectra of CDs prepared by different reaction media. Except for DCM-CDs, all other CDs have absorption peaks at 286–340 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the surface state CO/C-N [37]. The peak redshift of formamide-CDs is most obvious due to the rich pyridine nitrogen on the surface of b-CDs. The high energy absorption band at 260–270 nm is attributed to π → π* transition of the CC. EtOH-CDs and H2O-CDs have absorption peaks in the high-energy region at 216 and 236 nm, respectively, corresponding to π → π* transitions in the chained conjugated olefins and aromatic units of fluorophore. There are wide absorption peaks at 461 and 442 nm respectively, corresponding to the CN/CC absorption band of CDs [38]. To consider the effects of hydrogen bond, charge transfer, and dipole–dipole interaction on the luminescence of CDs, the absorption spectra of CDs prepared by different solvents were segmented. H-aggregates and J-aggregates correspond to the parallel stacking of molecules face to face and the parallel stacking of molecules head to tail. HJ-aggregates and JH-aggregates represent the H-like assembly of J-aggregates and the J-like assembly of H-aggregates, respectively [39]. The aggregation model can explain the solvent control phenomenon in this experiment. In the low polar solvent, the CDs only have weak emission formed by H-aggregation. With the increase of solvent polarity, polar solvents with hydrogen bonding ability can induce the increase of J-aggregates, and hydrogen bonds can induce the transition from H-aggregates to J-aggregates. This phenomenon is more clearly shown in Fig. 3 c. In addition, the trailing of formamide-CDs at 350–400 nm is related to the overlap of molecular orbitals caused by aggregation [40].

To analyze the influence of aggregation on CDs, the lifetimes of CDs prepared by different reaction media were tested, and the results are shown in Fig. S2d and Table 3 . The fluorescent decay plot of DCM-CDs is difficult to determine because of its low PL efficiency. Except for ethyl acetate, the CDs prepared by other reaction media all conform to the three-exponential decay kinetics. The short life component can be attributed to the sp2 luminescence of the carbon core, while the longer life component can be attributed to the oxygen-containing functional group or surface state luminescence. The latter promotes photon emission by filling substates or energy transfer before fluorescence attenuation, thus having a longer lifetime component [41]. As shown in Fig. S2d, the lifetime of EA-CDs is the shortest, and the lifetime curves of DMF-CDs and formamide-CDs are similar, which may be related to their similar chemical properties. The average lifetime of EtOH-CDs is slightly shorter than the first two, but the proportion of B1 of its short lifetime τ1 is significantly reduced, indicating that EtOH-CDs with higher oxidation degree has more surface or molecular fluorescence. The H2O-CDs have the longest lifetime, possibly because strong hydrogen bonds limit non-radiative recombination [42]. In general, with the increase of reaction media polarity, the lifetime of CDs also increases, indicating that the increase of media polarity will make the CDs tend to form more molecular fluorophores or polymers rather than larger carbon core structures, to improve the separation efficiency of electrons and holes.

To confirm the existence of aggregates directly, the absorption spectra of b-CDs and g-CDs at different concentrations were measured (Fig. S4, see Supporting Information). According to the definition of J/H-aggregates, molecular aggregation leads to a red/blue shift in the absorption spectrum, accompanied by increased/decreased luminescence [39]. As shown in Fig. S4a, as the concentration increased, the absorption peak of b-CDs at 340 nm was red-shifted to about 350 nm, which matched the definition of J-aggregate. In contrast, two trends were observed for the absorption peaks of g-CDs in Fig. S4b. With the increasing of concentration, the absorption peaks at 300 and 450 nm were blue-shifted and red-shifted, respectively, matching the characteristics of H and J aggregates, respectively. The emission spectra also confirmed the above-mentioned discussions. As depicted in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information), the increase in the concentration of b-CDs and g-CDs leads to a gradual redshift of the emission wavelength at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Specifically, the emission wavelength of the b-CDs moves from 463 nm to 528 nm (Fig. S5c), which accords with the basic characteristics of the J-aggregate, indicating that the aggregation caused by hydrogen bond may be an important reason for the emission of the b-CDs [43]. Increased concentration shorten the distance between particles, thus contributing to the formation of aggregates, further leading to energy transfer in concentrated solutions [44]. However, with the increase of concentration, the fluorescence intensity has a tendency to decrease, which is because of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect caused by too high a concentration. As shown in Fig. S5b and S5d, under the condition of low concentration, the g-CDs has two peaks at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm – the peak about 420 nm in the short-wave region and the peak about 500 nm in the longer wavelength region. With the increase of concentration, the short wavelength peak blue shifted and gradually disappeared, and the emission spectrum in the long wavelength region redshifted by about 100 nm, from 500 nm to 600 nm. These two peaks correspond to the behavior of H-aggregate and J-aggregate respectively. With the increase of concentration, the absorption spectrum of H-aggregates was blue-shifted, and the luminous intensity will decrease, while the absorption spectrum of J-aggregates will redshift with the increase of concentration and the luminous intensity will be enhanced. This suggested that the bright green emission produced at excitation wavelengths around 450 nm came from the J-aggregates of g-CDs. The redshift caused by the concentration of g-CDs (100 nm) is more obvious than that of b-CDs (65 nm) because g-CDs contain more carboxylic acid functional groups in polymer chains. The strong electron coupling caused by carboxylic acids leads to the creation of a zigzag polymer (J-aggregates) and a redshift as the concentration increases. However, the weak electron coupling of low sp2 hybrid fluorophores makes the emission bands of CDs change less in different concentration solutions [45].

The ratio, temperature, and time of mPD and TA were changed in order to investigate the effect of reaction parameters on the PLQY of CDs in the reaction with ethanol as solvent. Surprisingly, a series of changes in the reaction parameters basically did not affect the optimal emission wavelength of the CDs, which proved the stability of the luminous structure of the CDs. Specifically, using mPD or TA as a single raw material, green-emitting CDs can be produced, which proves that the benzene ring conjugated sp2 domain is the key factor determining the fluorescence wavelength, while N element doping, or the introduction of carboxyl group does not affect the emission wavelength of CDs. However, the ratio of mPD to TA has a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity. The area integral of the emission spectrum is used to measure the fluorescence intensity. Fig. S6a and S6d (Supporting Information) show that m-phenylenediamine plays an important role for CDs with high PLQYs. When mPD/TA = 2/1, the CDs have the highest fluorescence intensity. The proportion of the amino group is higher than that of the carboxyl group, which indicates that on the one hand, the amino group amides with the carboxyl group, and on the other hand, the excessive amino group also participates in the construction of sp2 carbon core to form graphitic nitrogen. It is also worth noting that TA can be oxidized by hydroxyl radicals to fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (λem = 423 nm) [46], but this peak was not found in this study, indicating that 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid was not generated. Fig. S6b-S6e show that appropriate temperature and reaction time can significantly improve the PLQY. Too high a temperature and too long a time will cause the CDs to over-carbonize, thus destroying their luminous structure.

The pH reversible fluorescence response of CDs is achieved through protonation and deprotonation of surface states or molecular fluorophore and energy transfer [47]. It is known from the previous description that it is very difficult to detect sensing capabilities in alkaline environments using changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. This may be due to the inability of sensor materials to maintain photochemical stability in strongly acidic and alkaline environments [48]. Therefore, CDs with high fluorescence stability over a wide pH range are very rare and have important applications [49]. The fluorescence quenching occurred obviously in the case of pH = 1, suggesting high pH stability of b-CDs (Fig. S7, see Supporting Information). The PL intensity of b-CDs (Fig. S8, see Supporting Information) decreased significantly under strong acid (pH = 1) and strong alkaline environments (pH ≥ 12), reaching 17.2% (pH = 1) and 19.4% (pH = 14) of the highest fluorescence intensity (pH = 7), respectively. However, the fluorescence phenomenon that is still visible to the naked eye indicates the excellent pH stability of b-CDs. This can be attributed to the fact that the structure of b-CDs protects its polycyclic aromatic-based luminescence center from direct interaction with the environment [35].

Under strongly acidic conditions, H+ on the surface of CDs will trap electrons and repel holes, thus interfering with the electron-hole radiation recombination of the surface state and increasing the non-radiation recombination. In Fig. S5b (see Supporting Information), the larger Stokes shift and FWHM at pH = 1 also verify this theory. The PL peak wavelength (Fig. S5a) of b-CDs gradually shifts blue with the increase of solution pH. In particular, the inflection points occur when pH = 4–5 and pH = 11–12. This can be attributed to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups (pKa = 4–5) and phenolic hydroxyl groups (pKa = 8–10) or amino groups (pKa = 9–10), respectively. When pH < 4, the degree of protonation of b-CDs increases, and the CDs have a more positive charge. Due to charge repulsion, its aggregation structure becomes loose, leading to the dominant emission of green fluorophores [50]. Fig. S9 (see Supporting Information) shows the PL excitation spectra of b-CDs in different pH solutions. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of b-CDs is more obvious under acidic conditions. This is because the defects formed by a large number of carboxyl functional groups on the surface of b-CDs trigger non-radiative transitions between different internal energy levels, resulting in different fluorescence of each energy level. When pH ≥ 4, the maximum excitation and emission spectra of b-CDs are blue-shifted, and the carboxyl group has been deprotonated, while the amino and phenolic hydroxyl groups still exist in protonated form. It shows that the functional group containing amino or phenolic hydroxyl group may have been the main luminous center. On the other hand, the rigid structure formed by the aggregation of fluorophores leads to the blue shift of luminescence. What's more, the deprotonated carboxyl group can form a tighter structure with the carbon core, thereby reducing surface defects and, in turn, increasing fluorescence intensity. When pH ≥ 12, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs decreased, and Stokes shift decreased significantly. This is possibly because the deprotonation of the amino or phenolic hydroxyl groups destroys the luminescent groups on the surface and increases the symmetry of the electronic structure of b-CDs [51].

The fluorescence intensity changes of g-CDs in different pH solutions are drawn in Fig. S10 (see Supporting Information) and Fig. 3. The pH response of g-CDs was similar to b-CDs, but pH does not affect the emission wavelength of g-CDs (Fig. 3a), indicating that the stable luminous center of g-CDs may mainly come from carbon core or aggregated molecular fluorophore rather than surface defects. However, the pH sensitivity of g-CDs in an alkaline environment is higher, especially in the range of pH = 10–11, the fluorescence intensity of g-CDs decreases rapidly, and the steep rise of FWHM indicates that the deprotonation process rapidly damages the luminous structure of g-CDs (Fig. 3b). This is due to the changes of charge on the surface of g-CDs caused by the deprotonation process and aggregation state caused by the reduction of hydrogen bonds [50]. The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs has a good linear relationship within pH = 10.00–10.78 (R2 = 0.969), so it can be used for precision pH sensing detection in a strong alkaline environment.

The sensitivity of g-CDs was 0.99/pH with a resolution of 0.1 pH. In addition, it was found by calculation that the pKa value of g-CDs was 10.34 ± 0.51 (Fig. 3c), which was higher than that of amino or phenolic hydroxyl groups. This can be attributed to the protective effect of the polymer shell in g-CDs on the amino structure, resulting in a higher pH tolerance. Fig. 3d shows the good reversible response ability of g-CDs, with no significant intensity change during 8 cycles within the range of extreme pH value. Table S2 (See Supporting Information) illustrates the comparison on the performances with literature reported articles. It was found that g-CDs showed the widest pH reversible range and the highest number of PL “on–off-on” recoveries, indicating that extreme pH values did not irreversibly damage the fluorescence of g-CDs, and that g-CDs could be reused many times [10]. Because of the high PLQYs, g-CDs were ideal to be used as pH sensors. The photo-stability in water, long-term storage stability, and the PL stability of the CDs were studied (Fig. S11, see Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. S11a and S11b, both b-CDs and g-CDs maintained stable PL intensity under UV light irradiation for 80 min. Fig. S11c and S11d showed that b-CDs and g-CDs could maintain their initial PL intensity when stored in initial reaction medium for 210 days at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S11e and S11f, it was obvious that the g-CDs were more resistant to metal interference compared to that of b-CDs. Only Fe3+ quenched 40% of the PL intensity of g-CDs, and other metal ions had essentially no effect on them. The above-mentioned experiments confirmed that g-CDs was able to serve as a stable fluorescent probe for pH detection in water.

Yellow-emitting CDs can be prepared by replacing mPD in the precursor with oPD. If the preparation temperature of the yellow CDs is raised to 220 ℃, the red CDs can be prepared. The CDs solution of all four colors can be mixed with water to form fluorescent inks. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CDs ink written on the weighing paper is transparent and invisible in daylight, but under ultraviolet irradiation, the CDs presents clear multicolor emitting characters (Fig. 4b). When the paper was stored at room temperature for six months, the characters were still clearly visible, demonstrating the excellent storage stability of the CDs (Fig. 4c). The color of y-CDs and r-CDs became lighter which was close to white. This was because the oPD-based CDs were more sensitive to oxygen in air and could undergo partial fading or discoloration [52]. More abundant anti-counterfeiting patterns can be obtained by encoding b-CDs and g-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4d, under ultraviolet irradiation, the pattern appeared as the number “38”, in which blue “12” was also visible, and the remaining strokes are green. When HCl solution (pH = 1) was sprayed on the surface of the weighing paper, the fluorescence of b-CDs turned green, and the number “38” in the pattern was all green (Fig. 4e). After spraying the NaOH solution (pH = 14), the fluorescence of g-CDs was quenched, and the fluorescence of b-CDs was restored to blue (Fig. 4f). This demonstrates the extensive information storage capacity of CDs and their significant anti-counterfeiting value, highlighting their potential for applications in data management and security.

Blue- and green-emitting CDs with high brightness and stability were successfully fabricated using mPD and TA as the precursors, formamide and ethanol as the solvents. The effects of precursors and solvents on the properties of CDs were tested. The PLQYs of b-CDs and g-CDs were 30.4 and 55.1%, respectively. Solvents during synthesis affected the morphology, structure, and aggregation state of the CDs. The b-CDs prepared using formamide solvent had short polymer chain, forming a tight aggregation structure and mainly J-aggregation. The g-CDs prepared using ethanol had longer polymer chain shells with the polymerization structure been relatively loose, as well as the coexistence of H- and J-aggregation. The change of reaction conditions did not change the emission wavelength of the g-CDs, but significantly affected the PLQY value. The b-CDs showed excellent pH stability, while g-CDs showed sensitive response in pH range of 10.00–10.78 with a sensitivity of 0.99/pH. To the best of our knowledge, g-CDs fills the gap in the accurate detection of CDs in alkaline media. Based on the differences in pH response ability of b-CDs and g-CDs, a multi-encryption anti-counterfeiting ink was prepared. Reversible fluorescence responses were achieved over a wide range of extreme pH values. This work provides new insights in preparation of multicolor fluorescent CDs with high PLQY, and further demonstrated that the choice of solvent in the synthesis of CDs was the key to the structure and fluorescence properties of CDs. And the CDs fabricated showed excellent potential in alkaline pH detection and anti-counterfeiting ink applications.

Yi Tian: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shuangyu Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported partly by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (grant no. 51972145), Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, China (No. 2021GXRC109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112262.

ce:section.ce:para:
